Chemical compound

**Berberine** is an [organic compound](/wiki/Organic_compound "Organic compound") classified as [benzylisoquinoline alkaloid](/wiki/Benzylisoquinoline_alkaloid "Benzylisoquinoline alkaloid").[[4]](#cite_note-hmd-4)[[5]](#cite_note-pubchem-5) Chemically, it is a [quaternary ammonia](/wiki/Quaternary_ammonium_cation "Quaternary ammonium cation") compound.[[4]](#cite_note-hmd-4)[[5]](#cite_note-pubchem-5)

Its name is derived from the [genus](/wiki/Genus "Genus") of plants, *[Berberis](/wiki/Berberis "Berberis")*. Berberine occurs in the roots, bark, stems, and leaves of *[Berberis vulgaris](/wiki/Berberis_vulgaris "Berberis vulgaris")* (barberry), *[Berberis aristata](/wiki/Berberis_aristata "Berberis aristata")* (tree turmeric), *[Mahonia aquifolium](/wiki/Berberis_aquifolium "Berberis aquifolium")* (Oregon grape) and *[Hydrastis canadensis](/wiki/Goldenseal "Goldenseal")* (goldenseal).[[4]](#cite_note-hmd-4)

Due to their yellow [pigmentation](/wiki/Pigment "Pigment"), raw *Berberis* materials were once commonly used to dye wool, leather, and wood.[[4]](#cite_note-hmd-4)[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-unijena-7) Under [ultraviolet light](/wiki/Ultraviolet_light "Ultraviolet light"), berberine shows a strong yellow [fluorescence](/wiki/Fluorescence "Fluorescence").[[4]](#cite_note-hmd-4)[[7]](#cite_note-unijena-7) As a natural dye, berberine has a [color index](/wiki/Colour_Index_International "Colour Index International") of 75160.

Plants containing berberine have been used in [traditional medicine](/wiki/Traditional_medicine "Traditional medicine"), and berberine [extracts](/wiki/Extract "Extract") are sold as [dietary supplements](/wiki/Dietary_supplement "Dietary supplement"). Other than in China as an [over-the-counter drug](/wiki/Over-the-counter_drug "Over-the-counter drug"), berberine is not approved as a [prescription drug](/wiki/Prescription_drug "Prescription drug"), regulated or proven safe in any country.[[8]](#cite_note-db-8)

## Biological sources

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Berberine&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Biological sources")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Chelidonium_majus_flores.jpg/250px-Chelidonium_majus_flores.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chelidonium_majus_flores.jpg)

Berberine is the yellow color of [celandine](/wiki/Stylophorum_diphyllum "Stylophorum diphyllum") poppies.

The following plants are biological sources of berberine:

* *[Berberis](/wiki/Berberis "Berberis") vulgaris* (barberry)
* *Berberis aristata* (tree turmeric)
* *[Berberis thunbergii](/wiki/Berberis_thunbergii "Berberis thunbergii")*
* *[Fibraurea tinctoria](/wiki/Fibraurea_tinctoria "Fibraurea tinctoria")*
* *Mahonia aquifolium* (Oregon grape)
* *Hydrastis canadensis* (goldenseal)
* *[Xanthorhiza simplicissima](/wiki/Xanthorhiza_simplicissima "Xanthorhiza simplicissima")* (yellowroot)
* *[Phellodendron amurense](/wiki/Phellodendron_amurense "Phellodendron amurense")* (Amur cork tree)[[9]](#cite_note-Cicero_Baggioni_2016_pp._27–45-9)
* *[Coptis chinensis](/wiki/Coptis_chinensis "Coptis chinensis")* (Chinese goldthread)
* *[Tinospora cordifolia](/wiki/Tinospora_cordifolia "Tinospora cordifolia")*
* *[Argemone mexicana](/wiki/Argemone_mexicana "Argemone mexicana")* (prickly poppy)
* *[Eschscholzia californica](/wiki/Eschscholzia_californica "Eschscholzia californica")* (California poppy)

Berberine is usually found in the roots, [rhizomes](/wiki/Rhizome "Rhizome"), stems, and bark.[[4]](#cite_note-hmd-4)

## Structure and biosynthesis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Berberine&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Structure and biosynthesis")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/BerberineBiosynEarly.svg/500px-BerberineBiosynEarly.svg.png)](/wiki/File:BerberineBiosynEarly.svg)

early stages in berberine biosynthesis

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/BerberineBinsynLate.svg/500px-BerberineBinsynLate.svg.png)](/wiki/File:BerberineBinsynLate.svg)

middle stages in berberine biosynthesis

Berberine has a [tetracyclic](/wiki/Tetracyclic "Tetracyclic") skeleton as is common for [alkaloids](/wiki/Alkaloid "Alkaloid") classified as a [benzylisoquinoline alkaloid](/wiki/Benzylisoquinoline_alkaloid "Benzylisoquinoline alkaloid"). The overall skeleton is derived from two equivalents of [L-tyrosine](/wiki/L-tyrosine "L-tyrosine"). L-Tyrosine is the precursor to [L-DOPA](/wiki/L-DOPA "L-DOPA") and [4-hydroxyphenylacetaldehyde](/wiki/4-Hydroxyphenylacetaldehyde "4-Hydroxyphenylacetaldehyde").[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)

The incorporation of an extra carbon atom as a bridge is distinctive. Formation of the berberine bridge is rationalized as an [oxidative process](/wiki/Redox "Redox") in which the *N*-[methyl group](/wiki/Methyl_group "Methyl group"), supplied by [*S*-adenosyl methionine](/wiki/S-adenosyl_methionine "S-adenosyl methionine") (SAM), is oxidized to an [iminium ion](/wiki/Iminium_ion "Iminium ion"), and a [cyclization](/wiki/Cyclization "Cyclization") to the [aromatic ring](/wiki/Aromatic_ring "Aromatic ring") occurs by virtue of the [phenolic group](/wiki/Phenolic_group "Phenolic group").[[12]](#cite_note-12)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/BerberrubineSyn.svg/330px-BerberrubineSyn.svg.png)](/wiki/File:BerberrubineSyn.svg)

[Pyrolysis](/wiki/Pyrolysis "Pyrolysis") of berberine gives "berberrubine", which [protonates](/wiki/Protonation "Protonation") to "protoberberine"

[Reticuline](/wiki/Reticuline "Reticuline") is a precursor to some protoberberine alkaloids in plants.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

Heating at 190 °C, berberine demethylates giving *berberrubine*. Alkylation of the resulting [zwitterion](/wiki/Zwitterion "Zwitterion") gives access to many berberine-like derivatives.[[14]](#cite_note-Tillhon-14)

## Research

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Berberine&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Research")]

Although plants containing berberine are used in traditional medicine, berberine has low [bioavailability](/wiki/Bioavailability "Bioavailability"), indicating limited biological activity [in vivo](/wiki/In_vivo "In vivo"), with no [patents](/wiki/Patent "Patent") issued for its use as a drug.[[4]](#cite_note-hmd-4)[[8]](#cite_note-db-8) [Clinical research](/wiki/Clinical_research "Clinical research") investigating the use of berberine in humans is limited.[[8]](#cite_note-db-8)[[15]](#cite_note-song-15) Although numerous [clinical trials](/wiki/Clinical_trial "Clinical trial") have been conducted or are underway, as of 2025, berberine has frequently been withdrawn as a [drug candidate](/wiki/Drug_discovery "Drug discovery"), and is not approved as a prescription drug in any country.[[8]](#cite_note-db-8)[[16]](#cite_note-hern-16)

A 2023 review concluded that berberine may improve lipid concentrations.[[16]](#cite_note-hern-16) High-quality, large clinical studies would be required to properly evaluate the effectiveness and safety of berberine in various health conditions.[[15]](#cite_note-song-15)

## Supplements, regulation, and safety

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Berberine&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Supplements, regulation, and safety")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Berberinapolvo.jpg/250px-Berberinapolvo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Berberinapolvo.jpg)

Berberine powder

Although widely available, dietary supplements have not been approved in the United States for any specific medical use.[[8]](#cite_note-db-8) The quality of berberine supplements can vary across brands: a 2017 study found that out of 15 different products sold, only six contained at least 90% of the specified berberine quantity.[[17]](#cite_note-funk-17)

From 2020 to 2022, the US [Food and Drug Administration](/wiki/Food_and_Drug_Administration "Food and Drug Administration") issued [warning letters](/wiki/FDA_warning_letter "FDA warning letter") to eight manufacturers of berberine dietary supplements for false advertising and misbranded drug products.[[18]](#cite_note-fda-warn-18) In the United States, berberine is not [generally recognized as safe](/wiki/Generally_recognized_as_safe "Generally recognized as safe") (GRAS).[[18]](#cite_note-fda-warn-18)

## Adverse effects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Berberine&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Adverse effects")]

Longer-term human clinical trials have reported [flatulence](/wiki/Flatulence "Flatulence") and [diarrhea](/wiki/Diarrhea "Diarrhea") as common issues.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

## Drug interactions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Berberine&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Drug interactions")]

Berberine is known to inhibit the activity of [CYP3A4](/wiki/CYP3A4 "CYP3A4"), an enzyme important to drug metabolism and clearance of endogenous substances, including steroid hormones such as cortisol, progesterone, and testosterone.[[4]](#cite_note-hmd-4)[[8]](#cite_note-db-8) Several studies have demonstrated that berberine can increase the concentrations of [cyclosporine](/wiki/Cyclosporine "Cyclosporine") in renal transplant patients and [midazolam](/wiki/Midazolam "Midazolam") in healthy adult volunteers, confirming its inhibitory effect on CYP3A4.[[20]](#cite_note-pmid34620272-20)[[21]](#cite_note-pmid22855269-21)[[22]](#cite_note-pmid25678418-22)

## Use in China

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Berberine&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Use in China")]

It is approved in China as an over-the-counter drug for [diarrhea](/wiki/Diarrhea "Diarrhea") treatment, with the [package insert](/wiki/Package_insert "Package insert") claiming efficacy against *[E. coli](/wiki/Escherichia_coli "Escherichia coli")* and *[Shigella](/wiki/Shigella "Shigella")* spp.[[23]](#cite_note-pki-23)

The Chinese package insert contraindicates berberine for people with [hemolytic anemia](/wiki/Hemolytic_anemia "Hemolytic anemia") and with [glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase](/wiki/Glucose-6-phosphate_dehydrogenase "Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase") deficiency ([G6PD deficiency](/wiki/G6PD_deficiency "G6PD deficiency")). The insert also specifically precautions its use in children with [G6PD deficiency](/wiki/G6PD_deficiency "G6PD deficiency") because it can produce [hemolytic anemia](/wiki/Hemolytic_anemia "Hemolytic anemia") and [jaundice](/wiki/Jaundice "Jaundice").[[23]](#cite_note-pki-23)
